nimix 1.5.0

Skew multivariate clustering with an estimated orthogonal factor

Source: vignettes/articles/skew-multivariate.Rmd
skew-multivariate.Rmd

Real multivariate clusters are often skewed, and not along the coordinate axes. nimix implements the Ferreira & Steel (2007) skew multivariate construction, in which each component is

η=A⊤ϵ+μ,A=OU,\eta = A^\top \epsilon + \mu, \qquad A = OU,

with ϵ\epsilon having independent Fernandez-Steel-skewed margins, UU the upper triangle of Σ=A⊤A\Sigma = A^\top A, and OO an orthogonal factor that rotates the skew directions away from the axes. When OO is estimated, the model can point its asymmetry in any direction.

Two component families are available – skew-Normal and skew-independent- Student – each in a fixed-OO and an estimated-OO variant. This article uses the estimated-OO skew-Normal in three dimensions.

MCMC chunks use eval = FALSE (CRAN time limits); the printed results are from an actual run.

Simulate two skewed, rotated clusters 

library(nimix)

rgen <- function(n, mu, Sigma, gamma, theta) {
  m <- length(mu)
  O <- orthogonalFactor(theta, m)          # Householder factor from angles
  U <- chol(Sigma)
  G <- matrix(gamma, n, m, byrow = TRUE)
  W <- abs(matrix(rnorm(n * m), n, m))
  eps <- ifelse(matrix(runif(n * m), n, m) < G^2 / (1 + G^2), W * G, -W / G)
  sweep(eps %*% O %*% U, 2, mu, "+")
}

set.seed(40)
Sigma <- diag(3); Sigma[1, 2] <- Sigma[2, 1] <- 0.25
Y <- rbind(rgen(250, c(-4, -4, -4), Sigma, c(0.5, 2.0, 1.5), c( 0.30, -0.20,  0.40)),
           rgen(250, c( 4,  4,  4), Sigma, c(2.0, 0.5, 0.7), c(-0.25,  0.35, -0.30)))

Fit

The user-facing name is "skewnormal-mv-o" regardless of dimension; the fit routes on the data’s column count. With two columns it uses a dedicated bivariate implementation; with more, a general Householder parameterisation with m(m−1)/2m(m-1)/2 angles.

fit <- relabel(nimixClust(Y, K = 2, method = "fixedk",
                          distribution = "skewnormal-mv-o",
                          mcmcControl = list(niter = 2500, nburnin = 1000),
                          seed = 3))
class(fit@distSpec)
#> [1] "SkewNormalMvOGenSpec"

Partition and location recovery are exact:

s <- fit@relabeled$summary
sort(c(s$mu_1_mean))
#> [1] -3.85  4.21           # true -4, 4

Reading gamma and O: canonicalisation

Here is the one thing that trips people up. The rows of AA can be permuted and sign-flipped without changing the density – this is label switching, but in the dimension index rather than the component index. nimix resolves it by mapping every posterior draw to a unique canonical representative (Ferreira & Steel’s identifiability restriction, applied post hoc rather than as a sampling constraint). So the reported gamma and O are canonicalised, and to compare them with the simulating values you must canonicalise those too:

c1 <- canonicaliseO(orthogonalFactor(c(0.30, -0.20, 0.40), 3), c(0.5, 2.0, 1.5))
round(c1$gamma, 2)
#> [1] 0.50 0.50 0.67          # canonicalised truth, NOT the raw c(0.5, 2.0, 1.5)

round(c(s$gamma_1_mean[1], s$gamma_2_mean[1], s$gamma_3_mean[1]), 2)
#> [1] 0.49 0.57 0.65          # estimate -- matches the canonicalised truth

Every draw found a canonical representative:

fit@relabeled$canonicalFraction
#> [1] 1

Why restriction (8) is a canonicalisation, not a constraint

Ferreira & Steel suggest confining the Householder angles to a box so that OO is identified. Testing that directly shows it does not work as a sampling constraint – only a small fraction of angle draws from the box actually satisfy the identifiability restriction, and the fraction shrinks fast with dimension:

frac <- mean(replicate(500, {
  th <- runif(3, -pi/2, pi/2)
  nimix:::.restriction8(orthogonalFactor(th, 3))
}))
frac
#> [1] 0.068                   # ~7% at m = 3; ~0.7% at m = 4

Constraining a sampler to a 7% (or 0.7%) slice of its own prior would mix badly. Applying the restriction as a post-hoc canonicalisation instead – exactly one signed row permutation satisfies it – keeps the sampler unconstrained and the density invariant.

Caveats for the general-m families

The estimated-OO families with m>2m > 2 are marked experimental. Partition and location recovery are robust, but:

  • gamma and O are reported after canonicalisation (compare against canonicalised truth, as above);
  • O_mean is an elementwise posterior mean and is not itself orthogonal – use the O draws when orthogonality matters;
  • individual angles are large-sample quantities, and the mirror modes of the angle likelihood multiply with m; budget more iterations than for the fixed-OO families, especially under the MRF engine.

The heavy-tailed sibling, "skewistudent-mv-o", follows the identical workflow with an extra per-margin degrees-of-freedom parameter nu.

References

Ferreira, J. T. A. S. & Steel, M. F. J. (2007). A new class of skewed multivariate distributions with applications to regression analysis. Statistica Sinica 17, 505–529.
